author: ROME | Earther [ROME]
content: The logic is intended to prevent people from linking a single pubkey (wallet) to multiple zProfiles. However, if you already had a wallet with a playerProfile in SAGE, your wallet is natively linked to that as a zProfile. While you can change your primary zProfile (xp tracking), and link/unlink wallets from various zProfiles, we don't yet have the ability to unlink a wallet with a SAGE playerProfile from a zProfile. But otherwise, yes, we will enable that zProfile un-linking in the future.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content: Wasn’t a style change it was placeholder at the start. Wasn’t really supposed to go out but didn’t have time to make the switch before launch. Almost every time people fall in love with whatever they play with first so there’s always has to be a PR campaign just to adjust expectations. Kind of like the crew cards where we had to preserve episode 0 and 1 art because people fell in love with the wrong roll chances. Even internally I try not to show wip art because the final art always has adjustments that work more holistically with the overall vision than what people are introduced with. For example, you’re taking the tier 1 option which is supposed to put players in an aspirational state versus something hand picked for the landing page. Also previous style lacks uniformity by its design as you can see the avatars were facing all different directions and placement in the compositions were random, the dot matrix pattern was not really a dot matrix and proportions were random. The previous style is also littered with AI artifacts. The new style is uniform and builds in tiers. As players unlock the colors changes, the lighting, effects and cosmetics change, the expressions grow and gives something aspirational to work towards. Tier 1 isn’t supposed to be top tier and is part of the design to motivate you to achieve something more full throated like the image you’re comparing to.

author: SCORMUD
content:
I don’t know if this has already been shared, but is it possible to get more details on how the Zink airdrop distribution works?
Is the allocation proportional to the percentage of ZXP one holds relative to the total ZXP pool?
For example, if a user has farmed 1 million ZXP and the total amount farmed by all users is 2 million ZXP, would that user receive 50% of the airdrop allocation?
In that case, the user would get half of the 10% of Zink being distributed — meaning 5% of the total Zink supply.

author: [SAI]Thomas94 [STAR]
content: Yes. Still roughly designed. But the current planning consists of a claim function that scales airdrop rewards over time, such that you can only receive 100% of your airdrop if you defer your claim until the end of that vesting curve. That time period would be something like 60-180 days (subject to change). For example, and these are arbitrary numbers right now, if you claim your airdrop on the first day, you'd receive 5% of your total airdrop. If you claim on day 180, you'd receive 100% of your airdrop. The balance "forfeited" by any user would roll back into the pool and would be distributed to people who defer their claim to later periods.  This is an update to a comment I posted some time last week, after @ROME|King Bryan-Titan Analytics shared some info on how another project is doing it. The initial plan was daily linear vesting with daily claim functionality. This smooths out the initial distribution of that 10% airdrop and helps mitigate the potential for massive immediate dumping.  We're not fully decided on the direction for this first season. The optimal strategy will accomplish seeding sufficient liquidity into the market to meet early demand for validators, staking, and network tx fees without creating a "pump and dump" scenario, and also not leading to an immediate dump. To that end, the linear distribution model might be preferential. But I do really like the gamification of the slash-claim function.

The initial plan was daily linear vesting with daily claim functionality. This smooths out the initial distribution of that 10% airdrop and helps mitigate the potential for massive immediate dumping. We're not fully decided on the direction for this first season. The optimal strategy will accomplish seeding sufficient liquidity into the market to meet early demand for validators, staking, and network tx fees without creating a "pump and dump" scenario, and also not leading to an immediate dump. To that end, the linear distribution model might be preferential. But I do really like the gamification of the slash-claim function.

author: [SLY] anthonyraEcon Vantarah
content:
It may just be me, but life happens and people who lock may need to unlock for many reasons. Penalizing them and rewarding those who are "lucky" enough to let it ride or die doesn't feel right. I get the entire sentiment and the goal of "gamifying" the game theory behind locking... hell this happens in real life with retirement plans and tax penalties. Just some food for thought

If you want people to lock their polis - may I suggest some sort of leverage/yield option instead. You lock because it will grow but not because of inflation (since you disable the minting of polis as a reward). You're not penalized when you want to use it yourself. It ends up being a solend like collateral system where you earn yield because the locker is used to fund xyz. I'm no expert, but if people are going to "store" something might as well have it work for you right.
